county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Moca, established 1772, carved from Aguadilla. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Moca was carved from Aguadilla in 1772 on the northwest coast. Known as 'La Capital del Mundillo' for its bobbin lace tradition. Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Monserrate holds records from the late 18th century. Pre-1772 records are in Aguadilla and ultimately Aguada. Sugar cultivation along the coastal plain and coffee in the highlands created dual agricultural records at AGPR. The mundillo lace tradition links to Spanish artisan heritage — craft guild and artisan records provide unique genealogical data. Barrios like Aceitunas, Capa, Centro, Cruz, Marías, Naranjo, Plata, Pueblo, and Rojas each have distinct settlement histories in notarial records.

source_profiles.source_name: Moca Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Moca municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1772.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.source_name: Moca Civil Registry (Registro Demográfico) — Births, Marriages, Deaths
source_profiles.repository: Registro Demográfico, Moca / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Civil registry records for Moca including births, marriages, and deaths from 1885. Spanish civil registration began 1885 under Royal Decree. US administration continued the system after 1898.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Civil registry records generally well-preserved from 1885. Some gaps in early years due to incomplete compliance. Tropical climate and hurricanes have affected some volumes.

source_profiles.source_name: Moca Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Moca district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1772. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Moca Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Moca. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Moca Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Moca district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Moca district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Moca Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Moca / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Moca. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Morovis, established 1818, carved from Manatí, Toa Alta. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Morovis was carved from Manatí and Toa Alta in 1818 in the north-central interior. Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Carmen holds records from the early 19th century. As parent to Ciales (1820) and co-parent to Orocovis, Morovis records serve a wider mountain area. Pre-1818 records divide between Manatí and Toa Alta parishes. Tobacco and coffee cultivation dominated — hacienda records at AGPR document agricultural families. The central mountain location meant families had limited mobility — intermarriage patterns are documented in parish dispensation records (dispensas). Check notarial protocols for property inheritance divisions that map family networks across barrios like Barahona, Cuchillas, Fránquez, and Monte Llano.

county_metadata.record_losses: Hurricane María (2017) caused damage to municipal infrastructure in Naguabo. Some local government records and documents may have been affected by flooding and wind damage.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Naguabo, established 1821, carved from Humacao. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Naguabo was carved from Humacao in 1821 on the eastern coast. Parroquia Nuestra Señora del Rosario holds records from the 1820s. Pre-1821 records are in Humacao's parish archives. Sugar plantations along the coast and coffee in the Sierra de Naguabo highlands created dual record sets at AGPR. Co-parent to Ceiba (1838), so early Ceiba records are partly here. The Cayo Santiago monkey colony (established 1938) brought researchers whose records supplement local archives. Playa Húcares area fishing families have distinct occupational records. Naguabo's Afro-Puerto Rican sugar worker families are documented in slave registers, civil registry, and church burial records from the 1800s.

source_profiles.source_name: Naguabo Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Naguabo including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Naranjito, established 1824, carved from Toa Alta, Bayamón. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Naranjito was carved from Toa Alta and Bayamón in 1824 in the north-central interior. Known as 'La Ciudad de los Prodigios.' Parroquia San Miguel Arcángel holds records from the 1820s. Pre-1824 records divide between Toa Alta and Bayamón parishes. Tobacco and orange cultivation (naranjito = small orange) dominated the economy — agricultural records at AGPR document farming families. The municipio's location between the coastal plain and the Cordillera Central placed it on historic trade routes. Check notarial protocols for land transactions along the Río de la Plata valley. Barrios like Anones, Cedro Abajo, Cedro Arriba, and Lomas have colonial-era settlement records.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Orocovis, established 1825, carved from Barranquitas, Morovis. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Orocovis was carved from Barranquitas and Morovis in 1825 in the geographic center of Puerto Rico. Known as 'El Corazón de Puerto Rico' (Heart of Puerto Rico). Parroquia San Juan Bautista holds records from the 1820s. Pre-1825 records divide between Barranquitas and Morovis parishes. This is one of the most isolated mountain municipios — families remained in the same barrios for many generations. Coffee cultivation was the primary economy — hacienda records at AGPR trace highland farming families. The Toro Negro State Forest area has distinct settlement patterns. Barrio Botijas was a center of rural mountain culture. Check notarial protocols for property divisions among large extended families.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Patillas, established 1811, carved from Guayama. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Patillas was carved from Guayama in 1811 on the southeastern coast. Known as 'La Esmeralda del Sur' (Emerald of the South). Parroquia Inmaculada Concepción holds records from the early 19th century. Pre-1811 records are in Guayama's parish archives. Sugar plantations along the coast generated enslaved persons records, while coffee cultivation in the mountain barrios created a different record set. The Lago Patillas reservoir displaced some communities — check relocation records. Barrios like Apeadero, Cacao Alto/Bajo, Guardarraya, and Muñoz Rivera document diverse settlement patterns. Check Guayama's extensive 18th-century parish records for pre-1811 family connections.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Peñuelas, established 1793, carved from Ponce. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Peñuelas was carved from Ponce in 1793 on the south-central coast. Parroquia San José holds records from the late 18th century. Pre-1793 records are in Ponce's extensive parish archives. Sugar plantations generated enslaved persons records — the 1872 slave register at AGPR documents enslaved families. Coffee cultivation in the mountainous northern barrios created distinct record sets. Co-parent to Guayanilla (1833). The petrochemical industry (mid-20th century) brought worker migration — industrial employment records supplement traditional sources. Environmental contamination issues created documentation of community health and displacement. Check Ponce's colonial-era records extensively for pre-1793 families.

source_profiles.source_name: Peñuelas Notarial Protocols (Protocolos Notariales)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Notarial protocols for Peñuelas district covering property transfers, testaments, powers of attorney, marriage contracts, slave sales, coartación agreements, and other legal instruments from 1793. Spanish legal tradition required notarized documents for most transactions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Notarial protocols at AGPR are generally well-preserved. Some volumes from the earliest periods show deterioration. AGPR's climate-controlled storage protects these irreplaceable records.

source_profiles.source_name: Peñuelas Spanish Colonial Census Records (Padrones)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan / Archivo Histórico Nacional, Madrid
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Spanish colonial census records (padrones) for Peñuelas. Include population counts by race, age, occupation, and household. Key padrones: 1765, 1776, 1800, 1812, 1820, 1846, 1860, 1887, 1897. Some padrones list individuals by name; others are statistical summaries.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Survival varies by year. The 1800, 1860, and 1897 padrones are among the most complete. Some early padrones survive only as summaries at the Archivo Histórico Nacional in Madrid.

source_profiles.source_name: Peñuelas Property Registry (Registro de la Propiedad)
source_profiles.repository: Registro de la Propiedad, Peñuelas district
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Property registry records for Peñuelas district. The Spanish mortgage law system (Ley Hipotecaria) was established in Puerto Rico in 1870, creating a formal property registration system that continues today. Records document property ownership, transfers, mortgages, and liens.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Property registry records are generally well-preserved from 1870. The formal registration system provides continuous chains of title.

source_profiles.source_name: Peñuelas Municipal Cemetery Records (Cementerio Municipal)
source_profiles.repository: Municipio de Peñuelas / Find A Grave
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Municipal cemetery records for Peñuelas. Municipal cemeteries were established in most Puerto Rico municipios during the 19th century under Spanish health regulations. Records include burial registers, grave assignments, and monument inscriptions. Find A Grave has partial crowdsourced transcriptions.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Municipal cemetery records vary in completeness. Earlier burials were in church cemeteries (camposantos). Some municipal cemetery records were damaged by hurricanes.

source_profiles.source_name: Peñuelas Slave Registers and Abolition Records (1872-1873)
source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Peñuelas including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. Also includes coartación (self-purchase) agreements, liberto (freed persons) records, and slave sale documents in notarial protocols. Essential for tracing African-descended families.
source_profiles.survival_notes: The 1872 slave register is generally well-preserved at AGPR. Earlier slave records are scattered across notarial protocols, church registers, and estate inventories.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Ponce, established 1692, one of Puerto Rico's original settlements. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Ponce, founded around 1692, is Puerto Rico's second city and 'La Perla del Sur.' Parroquia Nuestra Señora de la Guadalupe holds extensive records from the late 17th century. As parent to Peñuelas (1793) and co-parent to Juana Díaz, Ponce records cover a broad southern region. The Archivo Histórico de Ponce is a major regional research center. Sugar and coffee generated massive hacienda records — Ponce's enslaved population was among the largest on the island. The 1937 Ponce Massacre records are at NARA and AGPR. French, Corsican, and Catalan immigration is extensively documented. The Museo de la Historia de Ponce and Museo de Arte de Ponce hold relevant manuscript collections.

source_profiles.source_name: Ponce Catholic Parish Registers (Baptisms, Marriages, Burials)
source_profiles.repository: Parish Archives / FamilySearch / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Catholic parish registers for Ponce municipio including baptisms (bautismos), marriages (matrimonios), and burials (defunciones). FamilySearch has digitized microfilm for many Puerto Rico parishes. Coverage begins circa 1700.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Parish registers are the primary genealogical source for Puerto Rico. Some early volumes deteriorated due to tropical climate. FamilySearch microfilm provides backup copies.

source_profiles.repository: Archivo General de Puerto Rico (AGPR), San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Slave registers (registros de esclavos) for Ponce including the 1872 census of enslaved persons mandated before the 1873 Abolition Law. source_profiles.source_name: Ponce Military Records — Spanish Colonial and US Period
source_profiles.repository: NARA, Washington DC / College Park / AGPR, San Juan
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Military records for Ponce including Spanish colonial militia rolls (padrones de milicias), Spanish-American War records (1898), US military installation records, and personnel files. Spanish military records at AGPR and Archivo General Militar de Segovia (Spain).
source_profiles.survival_notes: Spanish colonial military records survive at AGPR and in Spanish archives. US military records at NARA are generally complete from 1898.

source_profiles.source_name: Ponce Historical Newspapers
source_profiles.repository: Colección Puertorriqueña, UPR / Biblioteca Nacional / Chronicling America
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Historical newspapers published in or covering Ponce. Puerto Rico's newspaper tradition began in the early 19th century. Papers include birth/death/marriage announcements, legal notices, estate settlements, and community news. La Gaceta de Puerto Rico (official gazette) published government notices.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Some newspaper runs are incomplete. The Colección Puertorriqueña at UPR Río Piedras has the most extensive holdings. Chronicling America has digitized selected Puerto Rico newspapers.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Quebradillas, established 1823, carved from Camuy. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Quebradillas was carved from Camuy in 1823 on the north coast (grandparent: Arecibo). Known as 'La Ciudad Pirata.' Parroquia San Rafael Arcángel holds records from the 1820s. Pre-1823 records are in Camuy and ultimately Arecibo's parish archives. Dairy farming and sugar cultivation generated agricultural records at AGPR. Quebradillas' coastal cliffs created isolated barrios with distinct settlement patterns. The Guajataca tunnel area connects to Isabela — families in border barrios appear in both jurisdictions' records. Canary Islander and Spanish settlers are documented in colonial church registers. Check notarial protocols for the complex land transactions in the cliff-side barrios.

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Municipio of Rincón, established 1771, carved from Aguada. Puerto Rico is a US territory (since 1898); previously a Spanish colony from 1508. Each municipio functions as a county-equivalent jurisdiction.
county_metadata.research_tips: Rincón was carved from Aguada in 1771 on the westernmost point of Puerto Rico. Known for surfing and whale watching. Parroquia Santa Rosa de Lima holds records from the 1770s. Pre-1771 records are in Aguada, one of PR's oldest parishes. Sugar cultivation and later fishing dominated — hacienda and maritime records at AGPR are primary sources. The BONUS nuclear reactor (1960-1968) brought scientists and technicians — AEC/ERDA records at NARA document this unusual community. Corsican and Mallorcan immigrants settled here in the 19th century. Rincón's position as the westernmost point made it a navigational landmark — lighthouse and maritime records provide unique genealogical data.
